choiceresponse.label: Check the Lime explainer output for the second sentence: “Thanks for your purchase. If anything looks incorrect, reply to this email and we’ll help.” Why does this message receive a high spam probability according to LIME? (Select all that apply.)

checkboxgroup.choice: Words like “purchase” and “reply” strongly support the spam class.
The word “Thanks” contributes toward ham but is outweighed by other terms.
The model assigns spam mainly because the sentence contains multiple sentences.
The LIME explanation alone is sufficient to determine whether the message is truly spam in the real world.
The explanation shows conflicting signals resolved in favor of spam.

div.p: The LIME explainer highlights specific words in the sentence that push the model’s prediction toward spam, most notably “purchase” and “reply”, which are commonly associated with transactional or promotional messages. At the same time, “Thanks” contributes toward the ham class, but its influence is smaller in magnitude. The model’s final prediction reflects the combined effect of these opposing word-level contributions, with the spam-supporting words dominating. Importantly, LIME explains how the model arrived at this prediction for this specific sentence; it does not judge whether the message is truly spam in a real-world sense or rely on sentence structure such as the number of sentences.
